# Why nutrition matters

> *The motivational gateway to the course. It answers one question — why should a
> medical student take nutrition seriously? — and frames the answer through
> the six pillars of health, with nutrition as the integrating hub.*

## The shape of modern disease

For most of medicine's history, the doctor's enemy was the acute and the infectious:
pneumonia, sepsis, trauma. That enemy has not gone away — but it has been overtaken.
Today, **non-communicable diseases (NCDs)** — cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes,
cancers, chronic respiratory disease — cause around **three-quarters of all deaths
worldwide** [@who2020diet]. These are, in large part, **slow diseases of daily living**, driven by a
handful of *modifiable* behaviours: what we eat, how much we move, whether we sleep,
smoke, or drink.

Diet sits near the top of that list. The Global Burden of Disease study attributes around
**11 million deaths a year** to dietary risks — too much sodium, too few whole grains, too
little fruit — making diet one of the single largest *modifiable* contributors to early
death [@gbd2019diet].

::: {.callout-note}
## Read the headline carefully
The "11 million deaths" figure is a *modelled* estimate from observational data, and its
large weighting on sodium is methodologically debated. The honest claim is *"diet is among
the leading modifiable risks"* — not a precise body count. We will practise this kind of
careful reading throughout the book.
:::

This reframes the job. A clinician who can only treat the acute crisis arrives, by
definition, *late*. The larger opportunity — and the harder skill — is to **prevent and
slow** chronic disease. Nutrition is where that work begins.

## The six pillars of health

Protecting health is not a vague instruction to "live better." It rests on a small set of
evidence-based behaviours that prevent, treat, and sometimes reverse chronic disease
[@aclm2022comp; @lianov2010] — used **alongside** drugs and surgery, never instead of them.
There are **six pillars**:

1. **Nutrition** — a predominantly whole-food, plant-rich eating pattern.
2. **Physical activity** — regular movement across the week.
3. **Restorative sleep** — adequate quantity and quality.
4. **Stress management** — recognising and regulating chronic stress.
5. **Positive social connection** — relationships and community.
6. **Avoidance of risky substances** — tobacco, excess alcohol.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prevention, the Singapore way
In Singapore, this preventive, behaviour-first approach is increasingly the *organising
frame* of primary care — most visibly through **Healthier SG**, the national shift from
reactive hospital care toward proactive, relationship-based prevention [@mohHealthierSG].
The conviction behind **Health in Medicine** is simple: helping people live well is core
clinical work, not an optional extra.
:::

## Nutrition as the hub

Of the six pillars, nutrition is special — not because it matters *more* in every case,
but because it **connects to all the others**, in both directions. It is the natural spine
of this course because pulling on it tends to move the whole web.

<div data-island="pillars-hub" data-src="/diagrams/pillars-hub.yml"></div>

- **Nutrition ↔ physical activity.** Diet and movement act together on weight and glucose;
  neither alone is as effective as both.
- **Nutrition ↔ sleep.** Short sleep lowers insulin sensitivity and raises appetite and
  intake of energy-dense food, while late, heavy meals worsen sleep — a self-reinforcing
  loop [@sleepRestriction].
- **Nutrition ↔ stress.** Acute and chronic stress (the cortisol pathway) are thought to drive
  "comfort eating" of palatable, energy-dense food [@dallman2003].
- **Nutrition ↔ risky substances.** Alcohol is itself dietary — empty energy that also
  disinhibits food choice.
- **Nutrition ↔ social connection.** Eating is profoundly social: family meals, communal
  hawker eating, festive food. Social context can entrench *or* transform a diet.

The teaching consequence is simple and recurs all year: **start with nutrition, but plan
across the pillars.** Fixing the diet often means touching sleep and stress too.

## Patterns over nutrients

A persistent trap — for patients *and* doctors — is **single-nutrient reductionism**: the
search for the one villain (carbs, fat, gluten) or the one "superfood." The evidence does
not support it. What predicts health is the **overall dietary pattern**, not isolated
nutrients [@aha2021diet]. Two of the most-studied patterns illustrate the point:

- The **Mediterranean** pattern (olive oil, nuts, legumes, fish, vegetables) was associated
  with about a **30% lower risk** on a composite cardiovascular endpoint in the PREDIMED
  trial [@estruch2018predimed].
- The **DASH** pattern (rich in fruit, vegetables, low-fat dairy) lowered blood pressure
  meaningfully, even without weight loss or salt restriction [@appel1997dash].

::: {.callout-warning}
## Landmark trials come with caveats — always
We name these trials as *teasers*; you will meet their limits in the clinical chapters. In
brief: PREDIMED's headline comes from a **2018 re-analysis** after the original was
withdrawn for randomisation problems, it was partly **cluster-randomised**, and the
composite endpoint was **stroke-dominated**. DASH was a short-term **controlled-feeding**
study with a **surrogate** outcome (blood pressure). Impressive — but not the same as
"prevents heart attacks." Quoting a trial *without* its caveat is a habit to unlearn now.
:::

## Why this is Singapore's fight

Diet-related disease is a declared national priority. The **War on Diabetes** (launched
2016) treats diabetes as a population-scale problem [@mohWarOnDiabetes]: roughly **1 in 3**
Singaporeans has a lifetime risk of diabetes, with higher risk among Malay and Indian
residents.

The **National Nutrition Survey 2022** shows why the thresholds matter — the population
diet is some distance from the guidelines [@mohNNS2022]:

| Finding (NNS 2022) | What it means |
|---|---|
| Only **~4%** of staples eaten are wholegrain | A *quality* gap (refined rice and noodles dominate) |
| **~9 in 10** residents exceed the WHO sodium limit | Most sodium hides in sauces, broths, and food eaten out |

Singapore translates guidelines into tools you will use as counselling anchors all year:

- **My Healthy Plate** — the "**quarter, quarter, half**": ¼ wholegrains, ¼ protein,
  ½ fruit and vegetables [@hpbHealthyPlate].
- **Nutri-Grade** — the A–D front-of-pack grade on drinks, by sugar and saturated fat;
  the *teh tarik* and bubble-tea labels you already recognise [@hpbNutriGrade].

These let advice stay concrete and local: a *cai png* (economy rice) plate becomes "two
veg, one protein, less gravy"; a Grade-D drink becomes *kopi-o kosong* or plain water.

## A learnable, examinable clinical skill

If nutrition matters this much, why is it so often neglected in the clinic? Largely because
doctors are **under-trained** in it: graduates across many countries report feeling
under-prepared to give dietary advice [@crowley2019]. That gap is precisely why this course
exists. Nutrition counselling is a **core, examinable, learnable** competency — and a
*behaviour* you can assess and help change [@frost2018mi], not a fixed biological given or a
test of the patient's willpower.

::: {.callout-important}
## What this year will, and won't, do
**Will:** build your nutritional literacy — the science, the Singapore context, and the
skill of advising real patients well. **Won't (yet):** turn you into a dietitian or hand
you prescribing protocols. Detailed medical nutrition therapy, drug choices, and disease
management come later — and you will learn *when to refer*. At this stage, nutrition is about
**motivation, concepts, and the clinical conversation**, taught with humility about what
the evidence does and does not show.
:::

## So — why should a doctor care about food?

Because this is where the largest, most *modifiable* share of modern disease is decided — and
because, unlike much of medicine, it is something you can actually help a patient change. Diet is
among the leading modifiable risks for the diseases that now cause most death; it pulls on every
other pillar of health, so improving it tends to move the whole web; and advising on it well is a
**learnable, examinable clinical skill**, not a matter of the patient's willpower. Taking nutrition
seriously is not a soft add-on to clinical work — it *is* clinical work. The rest of this book builds
the literacy, the Singapore context, and the conversation to do it well.

## Check your understanding

A quick self-test on the framing above — your answers are saved locally in your browser.

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/why-nutrition-matters.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: mapping the pillars

Concepts become useful at the bedside. Meet **Mr Tan**, who feels "basically fine" — and
practise seeing the whole picture, not just the plate. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/pillar-mapping-mr-tan.case.yml"></div>
